([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): Εθνικό Αρχαιολογικό Μουσείο, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Greek "Romanization of Greek"): *Ethnikó Archaiologikó Mouseío*) in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") houses some of the most important artifacts from a variety of archaeological locations around [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") from [prehistory](/wiki/Prehistory "Prehistory") to [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity"). It is considered one of the greatest museums in the world and contains the richest collection of Greek Antiquity artifacts worldwide.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is situated in the [Exarcheia](/wiki/Exarcheia "Exarcheia") area in central Athens between Epirus Street, Bouboulinas Street and Tositsas Street while its entrance is on the [Patission Street](/wiki/Patission_Street "Patission Street"), adjacent to the historical building of the [National Technical University of Athens](/wiki/National_Technical_University_of_Athens "National Technical University of Athens").

The **National Archaeological Museum** ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): Εθνικό Αρχαιολογικό Μουσείο, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Greek "Romanization of Greek"): *Ethnikó Archaiologikó Mouseío*) in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") houses some of the most important artifacts from a variety of archaeological locations around [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") from [prehistory](/wiki/Prehistory "Prehistory") to [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity"). It is considered one of the greatest museums in the world and contains the richest collection of Greek Antiquity artifacts worldwide.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is situated in the [Exarcheia](/wiki/Exarcheia "Exarcheia") area in central Athens between Epirus Street, Bouboulinas Street and Tositsas Street while its entrance is on the [Patission Street](/wiki/Patission_Street "Patission Street"), adjacent to the historical building of the [National Technical University of Athens](/wiki/National_Technical_University_of_Athens "National Technical University of Athens").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/National_Museum_6.jpg/330px-National_Museum_6.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Museum_6.jpg)

The Museum in 1893

The first national archaeological museum in Greece was established by the governor of Greece, [Ioannis Kapodistrias](/wiki/Ioannis_Kapodistrias "Ioannis Kapodistrias"), in [Aigina](/wiki/Aigina "Aigina") in 1829. Subsequently, the archaeological collection was relocated to a number of exhibition places until 1858, when an international architectural competition was announced for the location and the architectural design of the new museum.[[2]](#cite_note-Raffy-2)

The current location was proposed and the construction of the museum's building began in 1866 and was completed in 1889, using funds from the [Greek Government](/wiki/Greek_Government "Greek Government"), the [Greek Archaeological Society](/wiki/Greek_Archaeological_Society "Greek Archaeological Society") and the society of [Mycenae](/wiki/Mycenae "Mycenae"). Major benefactors were [Eleni Tositsa](/wiki/Eleni_Tositsa "Eleni Tositsa") who donated the land for the building of the museum, and Demetrios and Nikolaos [Vernardakis](/w/index.php?title=Vernardakis&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vernardakis (page does not exist)") from [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg"), who donated a large amount for the completion of the museum.

The initial name for the museum was *The Central Museum*. It was renamed to its current name in 1881 by the [Prime Minister of Greece](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Greece "Prime Minister of Greece"), [Charilaos Trikoupis](/wiki/Charilaos_Trikoupis "Charilaos Trikoupis"). In 1887, the archaeologist [Valerios Stais](/wiki/Valerios_Stais "Valerios Stais") became the museum's curator.

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the museum was closed and the antiquities were sealed in special protective boxes and buried, in order to avoid their destruction and looting. In 1945, exhibits were again displayed under the direction of [Christos Karouzos](/wiki/Christos_Karouzos "Christos Karouzos") and [Semni Karouzou](/wiki/Semni_Karouzou "Semni Karouzou"). The south wing of the museum houses the [Epigraphical Museum](/wiki/Epigraphical_Museum "Epigraphical Museum") with the richest collection of inscriptions in the world. The inscriptions museum expanded between 1953 and 1960, with the architectural designs of [Patroklos Karantinos](/wiki/Patroklos_Karantinos "Patroklos Karantinos").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## The building

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The building")]

The museum has an imposing [neo-classical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") design which was very popular in Europe at the time and is in accordance with the [classical](/wiki/Classicism "Classicism") style artifacts that it houses. The initial plan was conceived by the architect [Ludwig Lange](/wiki/Ludwig_Lange_(architect) "Ludwig Lange (architect)") and it was later modified by [Panagis Kalkos](/wiki/Panagis_Kalkos "Panagis Kalkos") who was the main architect, [Armodios Vlachos](/w/index.php?title=Armodios_Vlachos&action=edit&redlink=1 "Armodios Vlachos (page does not exist)") and [Ernst Ziller](/wiki/Ernst_Ziller "Ernst Ziller"). At the front of the museum there is a large [neo-classic](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") design garden which is decorated with sculptures.[[2]](#cite_note-Raffy-2)

## Expansions and renovations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Expansions and renovations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/0028MAN-Room3.jpg/250px-0028MAN-Room3.jpg)](/wiki/File:0028MAN-Room3.jpg)

The [Antikythera Ephebe](/wiki/Antikythera_Ephebe "Antikythera Ephebe")

The building has undergone many expansions. Most important were the construction of a new east wing in the early 20th century, based on the plans of [Anastasios Metaxas](/wiki/Anastasios_Metaxas "Anastasios Metaxas") and the construction of a two-storeyed building, designed by [George Nomikos](/w/index.php?title=George_Nomikos&action=edit&redlink=1 "George Nomikos (page does not exist)"), during 1932–1939.[[2]](#cite_note-Raffy-2) These expansions were necessary to accommodate the rapidly growing collection of artifacts. The most recent refurbishment of the museum took more than 1.5 years to complete, during which the museum remained completely closed. It reopened in July 2004, in time for the [Athens Olympics](/wiki/2004_Summer_Olympics "2004 Summer Olympics") and it included an aesthetic and technical upgrade of the building, installation of a modern air-conditioning system, reorganisation of the museum's collection and repair of the damage caused by the 1999 [earthquake](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake"). The Minoan frescoes rooms opened to the public in 2005.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In May 2008, the Culture Minister, Mihalis Liapis, inaugurated the much anticipated collection of Egyptian antiquities and the collection of Eleni and Antonis Stathatos.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 2020, there was renewed discussion regarding the need to further expand the museum to adjacent areas. A new plan was made for a subterranean expansion at the front of the museum.

In 2023, the Greek government approved plans for a 20,000 square metre underground extension to the museum. The extension is expected to be completed by 2028 and will also feature a rooftop garden.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Prehistoric collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Prehistoric collection")]

The prehistoric collection displays objects from the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") era (6800–3000 BC), Early and Mid-[Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") (3000–2000 BC and 2000 to 1700 BC respectively), objects classified as [Cycladic](/wiki/Cycladic "Cycladic") and [Mycenaean](/wiki/Helladic_period "Helladic period") art.

#### Neolithic era and early and mid-Bronze Age collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Neolithic era and early and mid-Bronze Age collection")]

There are ceramic finds from various important Neolithic sites, such as [Dimini](/wiki/Dimini "Dimini") and [Sesclo](/wiki/Sesclo "Sesclo"), and from middle [Helladic](/wiki/Helladic "Helladic") ceramics from [Boeotia](/wiki/Boeotia "Boeotia"), [Attica](/wiki/Attica "Attica") and [Phthiotis](/wiki/Phthiotis "Phthiotis"). Some objects from [Heinrich Schliemann](/wiki/Heinrich_Schliemann "Heinrich Schliemann") excavations in [Troy](/wiki/Troy "Troy") are also on display.

#### Cycladic art collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cycladic art collection")]

The Cycladic collection features the famous marble figurines from the [Aegean Islands](/wiki/Aegean_Islands "Aegean Islands") of [Delos](/wiki/Delos "Delos") and [Keros](/wiki/Keros "Keros"), including the *Lutist*. These mysterious human representations, which resemble modern art and inspired many artists, such as [Henry Moore](/wiki/Henry_Moore "Henry Moore"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) came from the 3rd millennium BC old cemeteries of Aegean islands along with bronze tools and containers.

#### Mycenean art collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Mycenean art collection")]

Mycenean civilization is represented by stone, bronze and ceramic pots, figurines, ivory, glass and faience objects, golden [seals](/wiki/Seal_(insignia) "Seal (insignia)") and rings from the vaulted tombs in Mycenae and other locations in the Peloponnese ([Tiryns](/wiki/Tiryns "Tiryns") and [Dendra](/wiki/Dendra "Dendra") in [Argolis](/wiki/Argolis "Argolis"), [Pylos](/wiki/Pylos "Pylos") in [Messinia](/wiki/Messinia "Messinia") and [Vaphio](/wiki/Vaphio "Vaphio") in [Lakonia](/wiki/Lakonia "Lakonia")). Of great interest are the two golden cups from Vafeio showing a scene of the capture of a bull.

#### Heinrich Schliemann finds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Heinrich Schliemann finds")]

Mycenean collection includes also the magnificent 19th-century finds of [Heinrich Schliemann](/wiki/Heinrich_Schliemann "Heinrich Schliemann") in [Mycenae](/wiki/Mycenae "Mycenae") from the [Grave Circle A](/wiki/Grave_Circle_A,_Mycenae "Grave Circle A, Mycenae") and the earlier [Grave Circle B](/wiki/Grave_Circle_B,_Mycenae "Grave Circle B, Mycenae"). Most notable are the golden funerary masks which covered the faces of deceased Mycenean nobles. Among them, the most famous is the one that was named erroneously as the [mask of Agamemnon](/wiki/Mask_of_Agamemnon "Mask of Agamemnon"). There are also finds from the citadel of [Mycenae](/wiki/Mycenae "Mycenae") including relief stelae, golden containers, glass, alabaster and amber tools and jewels. Other features include an ivory carving of two goddesses with a child, a painted limestone head of a goddess and the famous warrior's vase dating from the 12th century.

### Egyptian art collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Egyptian art collection")]

The [Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") collection dates back to the last twenty years of the 19th century. Notable is the donation of the Egyptian government which in 1893 offered nine mummies of the era of the [Pharaohs](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh") from [Bab el-Gasus](/wiki/Bab_el-Gasus "Bab el-Gasus"). However, the Egyptian collection is mainly by two donors, [Ioannis Dimitriou](/wiki/Ioannis_Dimitriou "Ioannis Dimitriou") (in 1880) and of Alexandros Rostovic (in 1904). In total, the collection includes more than 6000 artefacts, 1100 of which are available presently for the public. The collection is considered to be one of the best collections of [Egyptian art](/wiki/Art_of_ancient_Egypt "Art of ancient Egypt") in the world.

The exhibition features rare statues, tools, jewels, mummies, a wooden body tag for a mummy, a bronze statue of a princess, intact bird eggs and a 3000-year-old loaf of bread with a bite-sized chunk missing. The exhibition centrepiece is a bronze statue of the princess-priestess [Takushit](/w/index.php?title=Takushit&action=edit&redlink=1 "Takushit (page does not exist)"), dating to around 670 BC. Standing 70 cm high and wearing a gown covered in hieroglyphs, the statue was found south of [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") in 1880.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Stathatos collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Stathatos collection")]

The Stathatos collection is named for the donors and major Greek benefactors Antonis and Eleni Stathatos. The collection features about 1000 objects, mainly jewels as well as metal objects, vases, and pottery from the Middle [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") to post-[Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") era. Features of special note are the [Hellenistic period](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period") golden jewels from [Karpenissi](/wiki/Karpenissi "Karpenissi") and [Thessaly](/wiki/Thessaly "Thessaly").

## Artists and artifacts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Artists and artifacts")]

Some of the ancient artists whose work is presented in the museum are [Myron](/wiki/Myron "Myron"), [Scopas](/wiki/Scopas "Scopas"), [Euthymides](/wiki/Euthymides "Euthymides"), [Lydos](/wiki/Lydos "Lydos"), [Agoracritus](/wiki/Agoracritus "Agoracritus"), [Agasias](/wiki/Agasias,_son_of_Menophilus "Agasias, son of Menophilus"), [Pan Painter](/wiki/Pan_Painter "Pan Painter"), [Wedding Painter](/wiki/Wedding_Painter "Wedding Painter"), [Meleager Painter](/wiki/Meleager_Painter "Meleager Painter"), [Cimon of Cleonae](/wiki/Cimon_of_Cleonae "Cimon of Cleonae"), [Nessos Painter](/wiki/Nessos_Painter "Nessos Painter"), [Damophon](/wiki/Damophon "Damophon"), [Aison](/wiki/Aison_(vase_painter) "Aison (vase painter)"), [Analatos Painter](/wiki/Analatos_Painter "Analatos Painter"), [Polygnotos](/wiki/Polygnotos_(vase_painter) "Polygnotos (vase painter)"), and [Hermonax](/wiki/Hermonax "Hermonax").

Collections include [sculpture](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture") work, [Loutrophoros](/wiki/Loutrophoros "Loutrophoros"), [amphora](/wiki/Amphora "Amphora"), [Hydria](/wiki/Hydria "Hydria"), [Skyphos](/wiki/Skyphos "Skyphos"), [Krater](/wiki/Krater "Krater"), [Pelike](/wiki/Pelike "Pelike"), and [lekythos](/wiki/Lekythos "Lekythos") vessels, [stele](/wiki/Stele "Stele"), [frescoes](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco"), jewellery, weapons, tools, coins, toys and other ancient items.

Artifacts derive from archaeological excavations in [Santorini](/wiki/Santorini "Santorini"), [Mycenae](/wiki/Mycenae "Mycenae"), [Tiryns](/wiki/Tiryns "Tiryns"), [Dodona](/wiki/Dodona "Dodona"), [Vaphio](/wiki/Vaphio "Vaphio"), [Rhamnous](/wiki/Rhamnous "Rhamnous"), [Lycosura](/wiki/Lycosura "Lycosura"), [Aegean islands](/wiki/Aegean_islands "Aegean islands"), [Delos](/wiki/Delos "Delos"), the [Temple of Aphaea](/wiki/Temple_of_Aphaea "Temple of Aphaea") in [Aegina](/wiki/Aegina "Aegina"), the [Sanctuary of Artemis Orthia](/wiki/Sanctuary_of_Artemis_Orthia "Sanctuary of Artemis Orthia") in [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta"), [Pylos](/wiki/Pylos "Pylos"), [Thebes](/wiki/Thebes,_Greece "Thebes, Greece"), Athens, [Vari Cave](/wiki/Vari_Cave "Vari Cave"), the [Antikythera wreck](/wiki/Antikythera_wreck "Antikythera wreck") and from various other places in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece").[[2]](#cite_note-Raffy-2)

The museum houses the archaic terracota statuette [daidala](/wiki/Daidala "Daidala") that inspired the designers of the 2004 [Athens Olympics](/wiki/2004_Summer_Olympics "2004 Summer Olympics") mascots, [Athena and Phevos](/wiki/Athena_and_Phevos "Athena and Phevos").

## Library of archaeology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Library of archaeology")]

The museum houses a 118-year-old library of archeology with rare ancient art, science and philosophy books and publications. The library has some 20,000 volumes, including rare editions dating to the 17th century.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The bibliography covers archaeology, history, arts, [ancient Greek religion](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_religion "Ancient Greek religion") and [ancient Greek philosophy](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_philosophy "Ancient Greek philosophy"), as well as Ancient Greek and Latin literature. Of particular value are the diaries of various excavations including those of [Heinrich Schliemann](/wiki/Heinrich_Schliemann "Heinrich Schliemann"). The collection of archaeology books is the richest of its kind in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"). The Library has been recently renovated with funds from the [Alexander S. Onassis Foundation](/wiki/Alexander_S._Onassis_Foundation "Alexander S. Onassis Foundation"). Its renovation was completed on 26 May 2008 and is now named after [Alexander Onassis](/wiki/Alexander_Onassis "Alexander Onassis").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Access

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Archaeological_Museum,_Athens&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Access")]

The museum is accessible by the [Athens Metro](/wiki/Athens_Metro "Athens Metro"). The nearest station is Victoria Station (Line 1) and it is within a 5-minute walk from the museum. The museum houses a gift shop with artifact replicas and a café in the sculpture garden. The museum is fully wheelchair accessible. There are also facilities and guides for hearing-impaired visitors. It is next to the old building of the National Technical University and is served by bus, trolleybus and metro. It is not served by Proastiakos or the Athens Tram.
